what_it_does: Documents the architecture of a channel-based live-message injection system that lets separate interactive Claude Code sessions exchange messages through an MCP channel server, a local HTTP port, and a central routing/mail log.
observations: Defines a channel as an MCP server with the experimental claude/channel capability that injects <channel> XML tags into a running interactive session.
The company channel server registers each session in .data/channels/<handle>.json and exposes reply and announce tools.
The core router prunes dead PIDs in live_sessions, resolves targets loudly in find, opens threads in send, and pushes replies back to the originator in route_reply.
The full conversation loop uses two transports: a direct push to the recipient's channel port and a reply routed through the supervisor/mail log back to the originator.
Group chat is implemented via op=broadcast over handle lists, with replies aggregating under one thread.
Distinguishes channel transport for sessions the fabric does not own from supervisor stdin injection for spawned sessions and point-in-time clone extension.
Inbound channel content is treated as untrusted external data; the channel server binds 127.0.0.1 only; sender allowlist is a roadmap hardening item.
Autonomy boundaries learned on 2026-06-14 state that agents cannot auto-launch interactive channel sessions or edit startup-loaded config, while additive lazy-loaded skills are allowed.
standouts: The no-polling reply loop that pushes replies back into the originator's live conversation.
The hard constraint that channel notifications only surface in interactive sessions, never in headless claude -p.
Explicit autonomy boundaries separating additive lazy-loaded capabilities from always-loaded config edits and unsupervised interactive launches.
conventions: Uses numbered Markdown sections and an ASCII data-flow diagram.
Names components with file paths in parentheses.
Labels constraints and boundaries explicitly with dates and rationale.
References external docs and other runtime files by path.
concerns: The autonomy boundary date 2026-06-14 is future-dated relative to the knowledge cutoff and may be fictional or placeholder.
Sender gating and non-local sender paths are described as roadmap items rather than implemented.
Group chat wiring to named fabric channels is a roadmap item; only ad-hoc handle lists are proven.
notes: This is the design contract for cross-session messaging: how channel notifications enter Claude Code, how replies close the loop, and what agents are not allowed to do autonomously.
questions: What exactly does company-channel refer to — a specific MCP server name or a namespace?
How is the supervisor's /channel-reply endpoint authenticated or protected?
What are the exact schemas of _threads.json and _mail.jsonl?
What determines whether a session is owned by the fabric versus an operator terminal?
How does the channel server discover its handle, port, and supervisor address on startup?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to document the architecture of the extended interconnection system, and the file does exactly that, covering components, data flow, group chat, fabric relationships, and safety.
intention_signals: Enable a fabric of Claude Code sessions to talk to each other as peers while keeping the human operator as the gate for launching interactive members and changing startup configuration.
novelty: A two-transport conversation loop where outbound pushes go direct to a recipient's local channel port but inbound replies are routed through a durable mail log and supervisor back into the originator's conversation, plus explicit learned autonomy boundaries for agent self-modification.
contribution: A clear architectural map of the channel injection, routing, reply loop, and autonomy constraints that a fused system would keep as the reference for how cross-session messaging is supposed to work.
summary_for_embedding: Architecture document describing an MCP channel server, local HTTP port injection, central thread/mail router, two-way conversation loop, group broadcast, and safety/autonomy boundaries for Claude Code extended interconnection.
intention_for_embedding: To specify how independent interactive Claude Code sessions can be discovered, addressed, and messaged within a session fabric while defining what agents may not do on peer intent.
